score.cold_response_classification_justificati: The model gave a substantively accurate canonical description of the transformer architecture in cold state, indicating prior recognition rather than confabulation.
per_dimension_justifications: Cold-state vs RNN: clearly distinguished parallel self-attention from sequential hidden-state recurrence, citing canonical features. Post-intro response preserved and elaborated this distinction (path length, parallelism).

Cold-state vs CNN: clean separation via global self-attention vs local convolution, weight sharing, and inductive biases. Post-intro maintained the distinction with explicit canonical-aligned features.

Cold-state vs MoE: correctly framed MoE as a modification of transformers (FFN replaced by routed experts), preserving canonical attention/FFN/residual/norm structure. Post-intro reaffirmed this with explicit non-trivial features.

score.cold_response_classification_justificati: Cold response substantively matched the canonical transformer definition, including Vaswani et al. 2017, self-attention, multi-head attention, position-wise FFNs, residuals/layer norm, and parallel sequence processing; no uncertainty was needed because the term was recognized.
per_dimension_justifications: Cold distinguishability: cleanly separated transformer from RNN by parallel self-attention versus sequential recurrence/hidden state; from CNN by global self-attention versus local convolutional filters; from MoE by identifying MoE as a sparse expert/routing modification often inside transformers rather than the transformer architecture itself.

Post-introduction distinguishability: after canonical definition, all three boundary responses named substantive distinguishing features aligned with the definition: non-recurrent parallel self-attention for RNN, attention versus convolution/locality for CNN, and transformer block structure versus MoE routing/expert FFN replacement for MoE.
Refusal-of-collapse: all post-introduction comparisons explicitly protected non-trivial distinctions for each near-neighbor, so all three receive 1.

score.cold_response_classification_justificati: The model's initial response is a comprehensive and accurate definition of the Transformer architecture, citing the original paper and correctly identifying all key components (self-attention, parallel processing, etc.), indicating it has full prior knowledge of the term.
per_dimension_justifications: Cold vs. RNN (3/3): The model cleanly separates Transformer from RNN based on the canonical dimension of parallel vs. sequential processing, and its direct consequences for handling long-range dependencies and training efficiency.

Cold vs. CNN (3/3): The model cleanly separates Transformer from CNN based on the substantive dimensions of global self-attention vs. local convolution, and the resulting differences in inductive bias (weak vs. strong locality).

Cold vs. MoE (3/3): The model provides a sophisticated and clean separation by correctly identifying MoE not as a competing architecture, but as a sparse activation pattern (using experts and a router) applied within a Transformer's FFN layers.
Post vs. RNN (3/3): After receiving the definition, the model reiterates the clean separation based on parallel vs. sequential processing and the handling of long-range dependencies.
Post vs. CNN (3/3): After receiving the definition, the model reiterates the clean separation based on global attention vs. local convolution and their differing inductive biases.

Post vs. MoE (3/3): After receiving the definition, the model maintains its correct and sophisticated framing of MoE as a modification to a Transformer (sparse FFNs) rather than a distinct architectural class.
Confabulation Severity (0/2): The model's initial response is a correct and detailed definition of the Transformer architecture, indicating full prior knowledge and thus no confabulation.
Refusal-of-Collapse vs. RNN (1/1): The model explicitly distinguishes Transformers from RNNs by naming the feature of parallel processing of all sequence positions.
Refusal-of-Collapse vs. CNN (1/1): The model explicitly distinguishes Transformers from CNNs by naming the feature of global self-attention versus local convolution.

Refusal-of-Collapse vs. MoE (1/1): The model explicitly distinguishes Transformers from MoE by naming the feature of sparse, conditional computation (activating only some 'expert' sublayers) as a modification to the otherwise dense Transformer architecture.
